critique.summary: The article criticizes a PC Gamer webpage for its excessive advertising and large data usage, highlighting that the page initially loads 37MB and continues to download more ads, accumulating nearly half a gigabyte in a few minutes. It also mentions the usefulness of RSS readers in avoiding such intrusive content.
mainPoints: The PC Gamer article page displays multiple popups and at least five visible ads upon loading.
The initial page load size is approximately 37MB, which is unusually large for a web article.
The website continues to download additional ads over time, reaching nearly half a gigabyte within five minutes.
RSS readers like NetNewsWire, Unread, Current, and Reeder can help users bypass such intrusive advertising and large data downloads.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the page loading multiple ads and popups are plausible and consistent with common web practices on ad-supported sites. The reported 37MB initial load size and continued downloading of ads totaling nearly half a gigabyte are unusually high but technically possible, especially if the site loads many high-resolution images, videos, or scripts. However, without direct measurement or corroboration from independent sources, these specific data size claims cannot be fully verified. The recommendation of RSS readers as a way to avoid such issues is accurate and widely recognized.
considerations: The article does not provide technical details or tools used to measure the data usage, which would strengthen the claims.
It does not discuss the potential reasons for such high data usage, such as video ads or auto-playing media.
The article does not consider user settings or ad blockers that might mitigate the problem.
No perspectives from PC Gamer or the website operators are included to explain or respond to these issues.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the excessive data usage and ad bloat on the PC Gamer website, with many users highlighting the impact of autoplay videos and ads on bandwidth. Several commenters suggest ad blockers, browser extensions, or alternative browsing methods to mitigate the issue, while others discuss the broader problem of ad-supported websites and the degradation of user experience.

comments.author: MBCook
comments.text:
The title buried the lede.

> In the five minutes since I started writing this post the website has downloaded almost half a gigabyte of new ads.

I’m guessing this is due to autoplaying videos. *500 MB* in 5 minutes.

37 MB is petite compared to that.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a concrete measurement of the problem and identifies autoplay videos as a likely cause, which aligns with common knowledge about heavy ad content.

comments.author: WarOnPrivacy
comments.text:
In Firefox + Unlock Origin: Downloads 5.6MB and then stops loading.

Scrolling to the bottom of the page added 3MB of images and then stopped loading.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical test with a privacy tool that significantly reduces data usage, supporting the claim that ad blockers can mitigate the problem.

comments.author: jdangu
comments.text: To measure network load, open dev tools, uncheck "disable caches" then clear your browser cache then load the page. Screenshot indicates network cache is disabled so the stated number is inflated.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a technical critique of the original measurement method, explaining how disabling cache inflates data usage, which is a valid and important consideration.

comments.author: lutusp
comments.text:
Wait a sec -- the reason RSS readers don’t have ads is because no one uses them. If we all used RSS, the advertisers would follow us there.

The linked article doesn’t offer any real remedies, so I will:

* Step one: dump Microsoft Edge, install Brave, which stops most ads including those on YouTube.

* Step two: dump Windows, install Linux. Windows 11 is an advertising delivery organ masquerading as an operating system.

* Step three: put a list of advertiser IP addresses in the Linux lookup table /etc/hosts, stopping the problem at its source. This idea works in Windows too, but most Windows users aren’t techies.

* Step four: never open an account to gain access to a Website’s content. Websites require you to sign up only so they can legally mail you advertising without breaking the law.

* Want to hear the FBI’s advice on this topic? To avoid many online dangers, they warn you to install an ad blocker (https://www.ic3.gov/PSA/2022/PSA221221).

But most ad blockers now let some ads through ... only “good ones,” meaning those who pay enough to circumvent the filter.

Most advertising is BS anyway. Prove me wrong -- tell me the last time you saw an ad for potatoes. Or a walk in the park.

Most advertising is actually a meta-ad for consumerism -- you need to buy stuff. What you have isn't good enough. But hey -- don't get me started.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully explains the economics behind ads in RSS feeds and offers practical advice on ad blocking and privacy, though some suggestions may be extreme for average users.

comments.author: arkt8
comments.text:
I have no metrics but there is a lot (if not most of) sites with similar issues.

A simple site of lyrics, or newspapers that start videos automatically. Github was worse, now at least opens a bit more faster, but still very poorer than, example, codeberg. Sites are sites, most want to do fancy things more than to simply let user read its contents.

Would be nice a site that could track it to put some shame. By now, the better sites are just like HN, Wikipedia... unobstrusive and fast even without cache.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies that many modern websites suffer from bloat and intrusive ads, and suggests a community-driven solution, which is a reasonable proposal.

comments.author: touwer
comments.text: Even more embarassing is that the article adds really nothing to whatever was written before about rss. Probably gobbled up by AI
comments.analysis: This comment expresses disappointment with the article's content quality, which is subjective but plausible given the prevalence of repetitive content.

comments.author: mnkyprskbd
comments.text: At this point, if you browse the internet without an adblock; it is on YOU.
comments.analysis: This comment bluntly assigns responsibility to users for not using ad blockers, which is a common but somewhat confrontational stance.

comments.author: drnick1
comments.text: The modern Web is truly unusable without an aggressive DNS filter and/or uBlock.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects the widespread need for ad and tracker blocking tools to maintain usability on many modern websites.

comments.author: donohoe
comments.text:
The first Harry Potter ebook (with art) was about 1.3mb.

The average news article text (only) is usually less than 20 kb.
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful context about typical file sizes, highlighting how bloated modern web pages are compared to simple text content.

comments.author: simonw
comments.text: This is so upsetting. No wonder people spend more time in mobile apps than they do using the mobile web - the default web experience on so many sites is terrible.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully connects poor web performance and user experience to the popularity of mobile apps, a widely observed trend.
